metadata.title: Calibration Curve Auditor: When AUC Lies

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Bonjour, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?
props.segment: Calibration Curve Auditor: When AUC Lies

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Your model scores a ninety-two AUC and ships to the clinic. The radiologist sees a confidence score of eighty-five percent and trusts it. But the actual rate of true positives at that confidence level is fifty-three percent. That gap — between what the model claims and what it delivers — is calibration error. A high AUC tells you the model ranks patients correctly. It says nothing about whether the probabilities are true. This video shows you how to audit that gap with a calibration curve, and why the Brier score is the number you should be watching.
beats.visual_intent: title card: AUC vs calibration — 'high AUC, broken probabilities'; PROBLEM beat, no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to generate synthetic binary classification data, train a logistic regression and a random forest, compute calibration curves using sklearn, score each with the Brier metric, and label which classifier is overconfident.

props.command:
claude "Write a Python script:
(1) generate synthetic binary classification
    (1000 samples, 15% positive, sklearn)
(2) train LogisticRegression + RandomForestClassifier
(3) compute calibration curves (n_bins=10)
(4) compute Brier scores
(5) label which is overconfident"

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — read the output before running
beats.narration_text: Read the code before trusting it. The key line is one assignment: P-P-V per bin equals fraction of positives — that's what sklearn's calibration underscore curve returns. The Brier score is the mean squared error between predicted probability and true label. Two lines that contain the entire audit.

props.code:
# calibration_audit.py  —  Claude Code output
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve
from sklearn.metrics import brier_score_loss

# calibration_curve returns:
#   fraction_of_positives (empirical rate per bin)
#   mean_predicted_value  (mean predicted prob per bin)
ppv_per_bin, mean_pred = calibration_curve(
    y_true, y_prob, n_bins=10
)

# Brier score: lower is better (0 = perfect)
brier = brier_score_loss(y_true, y_prob)

# Overconfident: predicted prob > empirical rate at high end

beats.narration_text: Run it. The green curve is logistic regression — it hugs the diagonal, the model's stated probabilities match reality. The crimson curve is the random forest — at high predicted probabilities, the curve drops below the diagonal. When the model says eighty-five percent, the actual rate is seventy-eight. That's overconfidence. Same ranking performance, broken probability estimates.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_CalibrationCurve: calibration curves for LogReg (green, well-calibrated) and RandomForest (crimson, overconfident at high end) against perfect diagonal; Brier scores annotated; gap arrow highlighting the overconfidence zone

beats.narration_text: That overconfidence is fixable. Ask Claude to update the script: apply Platt scaling to the random forest, recompute the calibration curve and Brier score, and show the before and after.

props.command:
claude "Update calibration_audit.py:
-> apply Platt scaling (CalibratedClassifierCV)
   to the RandomForest
-> recompute calibration curve + Brier score
-> print before/after Brier for comparison"

beats.narration_text: Platt scaling wraps the forest in a logistic layer calibrated on a held-out fold. The Brier drops from point-twelve to point-nine. The curve moves to the diagonal. The lesson: calibration is a post-hoc audit, not a training outcome. High AUC is a ranking guarantee, not a probability guarantee. Run the curve before you ship the score.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'AUC ranks, Brier measures truth'; before/after Brier scores side by side

beats.narration_text: Your move: pull the predicted probabilities from any model you're shipping and run sklearn's calibration curve on a held-out set. Check the Brier score. If the curve bends away from the diagonal at high confidence, add a calibration wrapper before any human trusts those numbers.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run calibration curve on held-out set, 2) check Brier score, 3) wrap with CalibratedClassifierCV if needed

beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Your move: pull the predicted probabilities from any model you're shipping and run sklearn's calibration curve on a held-out set. Check the Brier score. If the curve bends away from the diagonal at high confidence, add a calibration wrapper before any human trusts those numbers.
